title: Research data: dataset, team and study
intro: Research data only makes sense alongside the study that produced it. This scenario branches on a single dataset vs a data collection, attaches the research team, and models the study as an Activity the data results from — the provenance link that makes a dataset reusable rather than orphaned.
question: Is a dataset a Record? How do I link it to the team and the study that produced it?

steps.prompt: One dataset, or a whole data collection?

choices.label: A single dataset
choices.why: Good — one dataset is one Record; its files (CSV, NetCDF) are Instantiations.

choices.label: A data collection
choices.why: Good — a collection of related datasets is a Record Set, each dataset a member Record.

choices.label: An Activity
choices.why: Not the data. The study that produced it is an Activity — modelled in a later step.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Fits. The dataset is a Record; its files are Instantiations; the study it comes from is an Activity it results from; the team is its creator.

choices.label: Instantiation
choices.why: That's the data file. The dataset's content is the Record; the CSV/NetCDF files are its Instantiations.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Too much for one dataset — a Set is for the collection.

capture.comment: Create the dataset as a Record.

choices.label: Record Set
choices.why: Fits. The collection aggregates related datasets — a Record Set, each dataset a member Record.

choices.label: Record
choices.why: Undersells it — a collection is many datasets. Use a Record Set.

choices.label: Record Part
choices.why: Doesn't fit — the collection is an aggregation. (A single variable within one dataset could be a Record Part.)

capture.comment: Create the data collection as a Record Set.

choices.label: Agent + has_creator
choices.why: Fits. The research group (and PI) are Agents. Relate the dataset to them with has_creator; reuse the group Agent across all its outputs and link individual researchers too.

choices.label: Author names in a README
choices.why: Doesn't fit for the catalogue. A README is fine documentation, but creators should be reconcilable Agents (ideally with ORCIDs) so their data is findable.

capture.comment: Create the research group as an Agent.

body.name: Coastal Oceanography Group, Univ. of Riverside

capture.comment: Link the dataset to the team with has_creator.

steps.prompt: The study/experiment that produced the data — how do you capture it?

choices.label: Activity, linked with results_from + performed_by
choices.why: Fits. Model the study as an Activity; relate the dataset to it with results_from, and the Activity to the team with performed_by. This is the link that makes data reusable — you can trace exactly which study, method and people produced it.

choices.label: A methods paragraph on the dataset
choices.why: Too flat. The study has its own dates, funding and outputs; as an Activity it links data, publications and people.

choices.label: Nothing — the dataset stands alone
choices.why: Misses the point. Data without its study is hard to trust or reuse; the Activity supplies that provenance.

capture.comment: Create the study as an Activity.
Link the dataset to the study with results_from.
Link the study to the team that performed it.

outcome.verdict: Dataset, team and study — reusable provenance.
outcome.summary: The dataset is a Record (or the collection a Record Set); the team is a reusable Agent; and the study is an Activity linking data→study→team via results_from and performed_by. That chain is what lets others trust and reuse the data — and find everything a study or group produced.

links.label: See real RiC data in the graph viewer
